es.text: Matar dos pájaros de un tiro
es.literal: to kill two birds with one shot
es.meaning: To achieve two aims with a single action.
es.example: Voy al supermercado de camino al trabajo, así mato dos pájaros de un tiro.
es.exampleTranslation: I stop at the store on the way to work — two birds, one stone.

it.text: Prendere due piccioni con una fava
it.literal: to catch two pigeons with one bean
it.meaning: To achieve two aims with a single action.
it.example: Se invito Marco e Giulia insieme, prendo due piccioni con una fava.
it.exampleTranslation: If I invite Marco and Giulia together, I kill two birds with one stone.

bridge.note: Spanish mirrors English almost exactly. Italian baits one bean to catch two birds — the older form of the metaphor, traceable to the 1612 Crusca. prendere (catch) is gentler than matar (kill).

review.notes: IT source is unusually strong — a scholarly Treccani article. Cue drafted by Claude 2026-07-20 from the entry's meaning and bridge — English situational framing, not a language claim, so it needs editorial taste rather than native review. Override freely.

cue: How do you point out that one action solves two problems?
